aliases: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

english: Din Tai Fung Hot and Sour Soup

region_of_origin: China (Sichuan / Shanghainese tradition); served in Singapore
food_subgroup: Soups — Chinese clear and thickened soups

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for hot and sour soup; A0CKM (Soups and broths) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept exists for hot and sour soup as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: seasoned with vinegar and white pepper

serving.natural_language_portion: One small bowl of hot and sour soup as served at Din Tai Fung, approximately 300 g, representing a single starter or side-soup portion.

nutrient_source.title: Hot and sour soup, restaurant-prepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite; cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo generic Chinese soup entries
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service; Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Din Tai Fung nutritional panel is publicly available. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for hot and sour soup (restaurant-type, NDB No. 06416 / FDC ID 172949) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Chinese soup category benchmarks. The small-bowl serving size of 300 g is estimated from typical restaurant portion norms for this dish category. Per-100 g values are derived from the USDA composite and adjusted for the known ingredient profile (mushrooms, tofu strips, bamboo shoots, black fungus, egg, vinegar, white pepper, starch-thickened broth).

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed soups and starch-thickened broths — analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database entries for starch-thickened soups and tofu-based dishes
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hot and sour soup in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI is estimated at 35 (low) based on analogous starch-thickened Chinese soups and tofu-based dishes in the database. The primary carbohydrate source is modified starch used as a thickener and bamboo shoots; both are low-GI contributors. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 12.6) / 100 ≈ 4.4, where carbohydrate per 300 g serving = 4.2 × 3 = 12.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~930 mg per small bowl serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this soup, as a single small-bowl serving provides approximately 930 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg — and should request a reduced-sodium preparation or consume only a partial portion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and moderate carbohydrate content (~12.6 g per small bowl)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hot and sour soup has a low estimated glycemic index and a low glycemic load per serving (~4.4), making it a relatively safe soup choice for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, though the sodium content warrants monitoring in those with concurrent hypertension or diabetic nephropathy.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from egg; low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (~930 mg per serving) is the primary cardiovascular concern; saturated fat is low (~0.6 g per serving), and the tofu and mushroom base provides plant protein and dietary fibre, but individuals with cardiovascular disease should monitor portion size and sodium intake carefully.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Original Hot and Sour Soup (Small) — known in Chinese as 原味酸辣汤（小）— is a classic Chinese restaurant soup served at Din Tai Fung in Singapore. It is prepared by simmering hand-sliced mushrooms, bean curd strips (tofu), tender bamboo shoots, and black fungus (wood ear mushroom) in a seasoned chicken or vegetable broth, then thickening the liquid with starch and finishing with vinegar and white pepper to achieve its characteristic sour and spicy balance. The soup has a smooth, silky, slightly viscous texture from the starch thickening, with earthy umami notes from the mushrooms and black fungus, a clean tang from the vinegar, and a warming heat from white pepper. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 35 kcal, 2.1 g protein, 1.0 g fat, and 4.2 g carbohydrate, with a notably high sodium content of around 310 mg per 100 g (approximately 930 mg per small-bowl serving). The glycemic index is estimated at 35 (low), making it suitable for blood glucose management. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium: a single small serving approaches 40% of the WHO daily sodium limit, which is significant for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or diabetic nephropathy. The dish is dairy-free and provides plant-based protein and dietary fibre from tofu and vegetables. It is not suitable for those with soy or egg allergies. In Singapore, this soup is a popular starter at Din Tai Fung and is widely recognised across Chinese-speaking communities as a comfort food with deep cultural roots in both Sichuan and Shanghainese culinary traditions.

confidence_reason: No official Din Tai Fung nutritional panel or HPB Singapore Foodo entry exists for this exact dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the USDA FoodData Central composite for restaurant-prepared hot and sour soup (NDB No. 06416) and cross-referenced with HPB Singapore Foodo Chinese soup benchmarks. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA composite is a well-established reference for this dish category and the ingredient profile is consistent with the described recipe.
notes: 1. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite for hot and sour soup. 2. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite for this dish. 3. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central composite for this dish. 4. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; no hydrogenated fats are used in this dish). 5. Serving size of 300 g is estimated from typical restaurant portion norms for a small-bowl soup; the actual Din Tai Fung small bowl weight has not been independently verified. 6. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct measurement for this dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 7. Halal status is listed as false because Din Tai Fung Singapore is not certified halal. 8. The food_id GD000605 is preserved from the input seed record.
